---
title: "Do Institutions Cause Prosperity?"
subtitle: "An IV tutorial: instrumenting modern institutions with settler mortality"
deck-author: "Carlos Mendez"
deck-author-url: "https://carlos-mendez.org"
institute: "Nagoya University (GSID)"
date: today
date-format: long
key-results:
  - num: "0.944"
    cap: "2SLS effect of institutions"
  - num: "+81%"
    cap: "larger than naive OLS"
  - num: "64"
    cap: "ex-colonies, one instrument"
format:
  revealjs:
    output-file: index.html
    # NOT embed-resources: RevealChalkboard (a core feature) is incompatible with
    # self-contained output, so the deck ships index.html + slides_files/ (Hugo serves
    # this fine — see the tutorial.html precedent). Figures are referenced ../python_iv_*.png.
    theme: [default, site-brand.scss]
    template-partials:
      - title-slide.html
    center: true
    slide-number: c/t
    menu: true
    chalkboard: true
    preview-links: auto
    overview: true
    progress: true
    history: true
    transition: fade
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    fig-align: center
    auto-stretch: true
    highlight-style: github
    # Math: MathJax (Quarto revealjs default) — renders $…$ / $$…$$ across the whole slide
    # DOM (body, captions, headings, table cells). Loads from a CDN; reveal.js is local.
    # Do NOT set `html-math-method: katex` — it is broken in revealjs (no auto-render call,
    # so math shows as raw LaTeX). See references/render-and-fix.md.
    link-external-icon: false
---

# The Tension {.divider background-color="#d97757"}

[Act I]{.act}

## Richer countries have better institutions — but a correlation cannot tell us which way the arrow points

Stronger property-rights institutions track far higher income across countries. The gradient is real and huge.

. . .

But maybe rich countries simply *afford* better courts. Maybe geography drives both. *The slope is correlation; it cannot prove cause.*

::: {.notes}
This is the central problem of the whole tutorial. Everyone agrees something about institutions matters, but the simple cross-country plot is contaminated by reverse causality, omitted variables, and measurement error. We need a research design, not just a regression.
:::

## Across every specification, the causal effect lives near 0.9 — well above the OLS slope of 0.5

![Coefficient on institutions ($\hat\beta$) across six specifications, 95% CIs. Orange = naive OLS; steel = IV with settler mortality; teal = an alternative instrument.](../python_iv_ols_vs_iv.png)

::: {.notes}
Spoiler figure. Don't explain every bar yet — just plant that the OLS estimate (orange, 0.522) sits well below every IV variant (0.7–0.94). We earn each one in Act II and return to this picture at the end.
:::

## Where we're going

::: {.incremental}
- Why OLS is biased here — reverse causality, omitted variables, measurement error
- The three conditions every instrument must satisfy
- Settler mortality as the instrument: first stage and reduced form
- 2SLS as a single ratio — and what the 0.944 number really is
:::

# The Investigation {.divider background-color="#6a9bcc"}

[Act II]{.act}

## Institutions are endogenous, so OLS does not estimate the causal effect

::: {.incremental}
- **Reverse causality** — rich countries can afford better institutions
- **Omitted variables** — geography, culture, human capital drive both
- **Measurement error** — the institutions index is a noisy proxy, attenuating OLS
:::

[A regressor is *endogenous* when it correlates with the error term; then OLS is biased even with infinite data.]{.comment}

::: {.notes}
Use the bathroom-scale analogy: standing on a scale while holding a weight gives a real reading, but it bundles your body weight with the weight you hold. OLS bundles the causal effect with confounding. We need a different tool to separate them.
:::

## The structural model: the error is correlated with the regressor — that is the whole problem

$$Y_i = \alpha + \beta X_i + U_i, \qquad \mathrm{Cov}(X_i, U_i) \neq 0$$

The outcome $Y_i$ (log GDP) depends on the endogenous regressor $X_i$ (institutions) plus an error $U_i$ that gathers every unobserved driver of income.

[The target is $\beta$, the *true* causal coefficient. The non-zero $\mathrm{Cov}(X_i, U_i)$ is exactly why OLS misses it.]{.comment}

::: {.notes}
U collects geography, culture, human capital, and measurement noise — everything we cannot model explicitly. IV will replace X with only the part of it that an external instrument predicts.
:::

## A valid instrument must clear three bars: relevance, exclusion, exogeneity

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### What an instrument $Z$ must do

- **Relevance** — $Z$ moves $X$ (testable: first-stage $F$)
- **Exclusion** — $Z$ affects $Y$ *only* through $X$
- **Exogeneity** — $Z$ is uncorrelated with $U$
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
### AJR's instrument

- $Z =$ **log settler mortality** circa 1700
- Deadly colonies became extractive; safe colonies got European-style institutions
- 1700 mortality cannot react to 1995 GDP
:::
::::

::: {.notes}
Relevance is the only one we can test directly. Exclusion and exogeneity are AJR's substantive claim — that settler mortality affects modern income only through the institutions it shaped. Picture a coin flip deciding who gets a drug: it changes outcomes only through whether the patient takes the drug.
:::

## The lab: AJR's base sample of 64 ex-colonies, one instrument, a 60-fold income range

::: {.incremental}
- **Outcome** — log GDP per capita 1995 (`logpgp95`), spanning roughly \$450 to \$27,400
- **Endogenous regressor** — protection from expropriation (`avexpr`), 0–10
- **Instrument** — log settler mortality (`logem4`), nearly six log points of spread
:::

[The `baseco==1` subset of the wider ~163-country world: 64 ex-colonies with valid mortality data.]{.comment}

::: {.notes}
Restricting to ex-colonies lowers mean institutions from 6.99 to 6.52 and the European-settler share from 30% to 16%. Australia, New Zealand, and the US sit at the low-mortality, strong-institution corner; Sierra Leone, Niger, and Mali at the high-mortality, weak-institution corner.
:::

## Relevance holds: a one-log-point rise in mortality cuts institutions by 0.607, F = 16.85

![First-stage scatter of institutions (`avexpr`) on log settler mortality (`logem4`), 64 ex-colonies. Slope $-0.607$, $F = 16.85$, $R^2 = 0.27$.](../python_iv_first_stage.png)

::: {.notes}
The first-stage F of 16.85 is just above the Staiger-Stock rule of thumb of 10 and almost exactly at the Stock-Yogo iid threshold of 16.38. Honest disclosure: this is borderline-strong, not comfortable — several robustness specs push F below 5, where the confidence intervals should not be taken literally.
:::

## The reduced form confirms it: deadlier colonies are about 30 times poorer today

![Reduced-form scatter of log GDP (`logpgp95`) on log settler mortality (`logem4`). The slope ($\approx -0.573$) is the *total* effect of the instrument on the outcome.](../python_iv_reduced_form.png)

::: {.notes}
Across the 5.8-log-point span of mortality, the fitted line predicts a GDP gap of about 3.4 log points — roughly 30 times poorer for the deadliest colonies. This total effect is what 2SLS decomposes into the first-stage and second-stage channels.
:::

## 2SLS is just one division: the reduced-form slope over the first-stage slope

$$\hat\beta_{2SLS} = \frac{\widehat{\mathrm{Cov}}(Y, Z)}{\widehat{\mathrm{Cov}}(X, Z)} = \frac{\hat\beta_{RF}}{\hat\beta_{FS}} = \frac{-0.573}{-0.607} = 0.944$$

The numerator is the total effect of the instrument on the outcome; the denominator rescales by how much the instrument moves institutions.

[The whole IV machinery, in one ratio: $-0.573 / -0.607 = 0.944$.]{.comment}

::: {.notes}
The rope analogy: if pulling a rope by 1 meter moves a hidden box by 0.6 meters and lifts a flag by 0.57 meters, then moving the box by 1 meter lifts the flag by 0.57/0.6 = 0.94. IV is this proportion calculation.
:::

## Two libraries, one formula: pyfixest gives the estimate, linearmodels the diagnostics

``` {.python code-line-numbers="1-2|3-4|5-6"}
import pyfixest as pf
from linearmodels.iv import IV2SLS
# structural 2SLS via pyfixest's "exog | endog ~ instrument" syntax
m_iv = pf.feols("logpgp95 ~ 1 | avexpr ~ logem4", data=base, vcov="HC1")
# weak-IV F, Wu-Hausman, Hansen J — the tests pyfixest does not report
res  = IV2SLS(base["logpgp95"], X_exog, base[["avexpr"]], base[["logem4"]]).fit(cov_type="robust")
```

::: {.notes}
pyfixest excels at the idiomatic formula syntax and the structural estimate; linearmodels supplies the Kleibergen-Paap-style first-stage F, the Wu-Hausman endogeneity test, and the Hansen J overidentification test. Each does the job it does best. Both agree on the point estimate to six decimals.
:::

## Naive OLS sees only half the story: a slope of 0.522 {background-color="#141413"}

[0.522]{.bignum}

[OLS estimate of $\hat\beta$ on institutions, base sample (SE 0.050) — the benchmark IV will overturn]{.bignum-label}

::: {.notes}
0.522 in the 64-country base sample, stable across specifications and significant at the 1% level. But it carries reverse causality, omitted-variable bias, and attenuation from measurement error. The question is how much of the 0.522 is bias.
:::

## The strongest objection — and the answer

[Objection.]{.objection} Maybe the tropical disease environment that killed settlers *still* depresses productivity today — a direct arrow from mortality to GDP that breaks the exclusion restriction.

. . .

[Response.]{.rebuttal} Adding modern health controls pulls $\hat\beta$ down only to 0.55–0.69 — still above OLS — and the overidentification tests (Hansen J, $p$ = 0.18–0.79) do not reject joint exogeneity. The threat is real but bounded; it does not erase the effect.

::: {.notes}
Steelman, don't strawman. Health is the tightest empirical challenge. AJR read modern health as a "bad control" downstream of institutions; a critic reads it as genuine exogenous variation. The data alone cannot adjudicate, but the coefficient survives — it never collapses to zero.
:::

# The Resolution {.divider background-color="#00d4c8"}

[Act III]{.act}

## Instrumenting institutions recovers a causal effect of 0.944 — Wu-Hausman confirms OLS was biased {background-color="#141413"}

[0.944]{.bignum}

[2SLS $\hat\beta$ on institutions (SE 0.176, 95% CI [0.60, 1.29]); Wu-Hausman $F = 24.22$, $p < 0.0001$]{.bignum-label}

::: {.notes}
The Wu-Hausman test rejects the null that OLS is consistent — the IV–OLS gap is statistical evidence of bias, so IV is empirically warranted, not just theoretically motivated. The point estimate matches the Stata ivreg2 reference to three decimals.
:::

## The causal effect is 81% larger than OLS — measurement error, not endogeneity, dominated the bias

| Estimator | $\hat\beta$ | SE | 95% CI |
|---|---:|---:|:--:|
| OLS (base sample) | 0.522 | 0.050 | — |
| 2SLS (settler mortality) | [0.944]{.key} | 0.176 | [0.60, 1.29] |

[IV > OLS by 81% implies attenuation from measurement error outweighed reverse causality and omitted variables.]{.comment}

::: {.notes}
Three biases pull in opposite directions: reverse causality and omitted variables push OLS up; classical measurement error in the institutions index pulls it down. IV landing 81% above OLS says the downward attenuation dominated — de-noising the proxy reveals a steeper causal slope, not a shallower one.
:::

## The 0.944 is a LATE for compliers, and it leans on assumptions only partly testable

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### What it is

- A **Local Average Treatment Effect** (Imbens-Angrist)
- The effect for *complier* countries near the colonization margin
- Not a population average for every country
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
### What still carries weight

- Exclusion is **untestable in principle**
- Albouy (2012): ~36% of mortality data imputed or shared
- Hansen J cannot detect coordinated witnesses
:::
::::

::: {.notes}
LASSO-free but assumption-bound: 2SLS recovers a complier effect, not the population ATE. Albouy's imputation critique limits what overidentification non-rejection can buy — shared imputation noise would let the alternative instruments agree falsely. Honest IV reporting names both the estimand and the maintained assumptions.
:::

# Let the disease environment of 1700, not the regression of 1995, identify the effect of institutions. {.divider background-color="#141413"}

::: {.notes}
The single takeaway. A credible instrument turns a contaminated 0.522 correlation into a 0.944 causal estimate — implying institutional reform is roughly twice as valuable as naive cross-country regressions suggest. The design, not the data, does the work.
:::
